title: She Changed Her Wallpaper
content: It started with nothing.
A Tuesday. The kind that doesn’t announce itself — just arrives, grey and slightly too loud, full of notifications and small demands.
She was sitting in a café, waiting for her coffee to cool.
She picked up her phone — out of habit, not out of need — and for a moment just looked at the screen.
The wallpaper was a screenshot of something. A note, maybe. Or a reminder. She couldn’t even remember when she’d set it.
It looked exactly like how she felt.
So she changed it.
It took less than a minute.
She scrolled through her camera roll and stopped on an image she had saved months ago — soft light through curtains, pale, quiet, unhurried. Something that looked the way a slow morning feels.
She set it as her wallpaper and put the phone face down.
When she picked it up again, something had shifted.
Not the day.
The day was exactly the same.
The café was still loud. The coffee was still cooling. Tuesday was still Tuesday.
But the first thing she saw — instead of a notification, instead of a task — was something quiet.
Something that reminded her, without words, that beauty exists. That softness is allowed. That not every moment has to be productive to be real.
I’ve been thinking about this ever since.
We spend more time looking at our phone screens than almost anything else.
We look at them first thing in the morning, before we’ve said a word.
We look at them last at night, in the dark, when we are most open to whatever they show us.
And most of the time, we’ve never consciously chosen what greets us there.
It seems like such a small thing. A wallpaper. A background image. Nothing that should matter.
But I don’t think that’s true.
I think small things are exactly where everything begins to change.
The image your eyes meet first — it sets something in motion.
A feeling. A tone. A quiet agreement with yourself about what kind of day this might be.
If you see chaos, you start in chaos.
If you see something calm, something beautiful, something that feels like you — you start from there instead.
This is not magic.
It is attention. Directed, gently, toward something that nourishes rather than depletes.
I made the Aya wallpaper collection for exactly this reason.
Not as decoration. Not as something to save and forget.
But as something you return to.
Every time you reach for your phone.
Every time the day feels heavier than it should.
Every time you need a small reminder that not everything has to be rushed.
That something can be quiet.
And still matter.
Because most of the time, we don’t change our lives all at once.
We change what we look at.
And slowly, it changes us back.
With love, Aya
